The Language class — Language validation¶

New in version 0.1.

Module author: Claude Gibert <claude.gibert@synopticview.co.uk>

class language.language.LanguageError(Exception)¶

Defines an exception class to catch at higher levels if the error has to do with language checking. Other exceptions can be raised in this module

class language.language.Language(object)¶

The Language class is not meant to instanciated, it implements class methods to read language definition modules and files and then apply these to a dictionary.

The Language class offers one single public method. This method uses three pieces of information to “do the job”:

  • a dictionary provided by the user which needs to be analysed and matched against rules (e.g. default values, required keywords, type conversions etc...)
  • a language definition defining all these rules
  • language definition rules which define rules for the language definition and default behaviour when information is not provided in the language (see the Language.LanguageDefinition.LanguageDefinition class).

This may sound complicated but the user should never need to look at the third component: the language definition rules. The second component is only known to users who design a language.

Public methods¶

resolveDirective(directive,directive_name,directive_reader):
Parameters:
  • directive (a dictionary or any object behaving like a dictionary) – the data to be checked against the language rules
  • directive_name (string) – a name which enables the directive reader to provide the language rules.
  • directive_reader (any callable object which returns the language definition from a single string parameter) – an object able to return a dictionary compliant with what is defined in class:: LanguageDefinition. It receives the string directive_name. This means that the language definition can be stored in any format, or even be a python module.

The class method resolveDirective() raises a Language.Language.LanguageError exception if any of the steps goes wrong. However, it continues as far as possible and only raises the exception with a list of all errors encountered to facilitate debugging. It follows the following steps:

  • retrieve the language definition through the directive_reader. This is a recursive process, if other language definitions are inherited, they are merged together,
  • check the correctness of the language definition(s) in syntax and grammar,
  • default language keywords which were not provided in directive are inserted,
  • aliases are substituted for the legal keyword
  • default values which were not provided are inserted
  • the presence of required keywords is checked
  • keywords which are allowed only one value are checked, keywords with more than one value are converted to lists,
  • for each keyword, if a type (or class) is specified, the value is converted,
  • values including environment variables are substituted with the value of the variable(s)
  • string values are converted to lower case, unless specified otherwise
  • at this stage an exception can be raised if at least one error has been detected in the previous stage.
  • if no error occured, then one last step is necessary: to run validators on values to make sure everything is ok, values can be derived etc...,
  • if not error is encountered, the directive cleaned up and checked is returned.

The errors generated by this method are:

  1. unknown keywords (not defined in the language),
  2. keywords with more one than one value which should only have one,
  3. errors while trying to convert values to specified types,
  4. errors while trying to substitute values with environment variables (environment variable missing),
  5. validation of the value(s) of a keyword failed.
Note :At this stage it seems more interesting to gather as many errors as possible in one single language.language.LanguageError exception rather than generating specialised exceptions for each type of error.
Warning :The directive sent as the first parameter is the one which is returned, it is modified by this method. If you want to preserve it, you will need to duplicate it before calling this method. This is done deliberateley to avoid the systematic duplication of the parameter if it is not necessary.
Note :The parameter directive is expected to have two attributes: default_ and keywords_. They should both be instances of the set class. On return they contain respectively the list of keywords which received a default value and the list of keywords valid in this directive.

Private methods¶

validate_language(directive_name,directive_reader):
Parameters:
  • directive_name (string) – name of the language definition (probably the name of the directive)
  • directive_reader (any callable object which returns the language definition from a single string parameter) – an object able to return a dictionary compliant with what is defined in class:: LanguageDefinition. It receives the string directive_name. This means that the language definition can be stored in any format, or even be a python module.

This method gets the language definition for a directive through the directive_reader, deals recursively with inherited language definitions if specified. It checks the validity of the language definitions and builds a final language definition from inherited definitions.

Returns: the final language definition, the definition of how the language should describe the directive and the definition of how the keywords should be defined.

inherit_from(parent,child):
Parameters:
  • parent (dictionary) – the dictionary to inherit from
  • child (dictionary) – the dictionary to inherit into

Inserts into child any entry of parent which is not in child. From entries which are in child and in parent, inherit all the entries in the entry from parent into the child.

specialize_from(parent,child):
Parameters:
  • parent (dictionary) – the dictionary to override values from
  • child (dictionary) – the dictionary to ovveride into

Inserts into child any entry of parent which is not in child. Replaces in child any entry which is both in the parent and the child.

validate_language_part(semantic,language):
Parameters:
  • semantic (dictionary) – the definition of how the language should be defined
  • language (dictionary) – the language to check

Checks that the language definition contains all the required keywords and ensures that the different entries are of the correct type.

Returns the language after modification.

resolveSpecialKeywords(definition,directive,language):
Parameters:
  • definition (dictionary) – A dictionary written in the language definition under the keyword specialize_from.
  • directive (dictionary) – the directive given by the user.
  • language (dictionary) – the language being used to verify the directive

This method tries to extract keywords from the different expressions in definition which are defined in the language. It then applies the language definition to those keywords in order to make sure that, before evaluating the expressions, the keywords exist (possible default values) and are of the expected type.

In order to apply the language to a reduced set of keyword, this method creates a reduced directive and builds in memory a reduced language definition.

find_specials(definition,directive):
Parameters:
  • definition (dictionary) – A dictionary written in the language definition under the keyword specialize_from.
  • directive (dictionary) – the directive given by the user.

This methods evaluates expressions defined under the specialize_from in the language definition. The syntax is as follows:

...
specialize_from = {
    "value1 == 'hello' and value2 == 'world'": "hello",
    "value3 < 12": "not_twelve"
}
...

Where the keys are boolean expressions which can name any key in the user’s directive and the values are the name of directive definitions. The contents of the directive definition is loaded and its keys replace the current keys if they exist or they are added. If the evaluation of the expression if False, nothing is done.

This is useful to define the semantics of directive depending on values of some of its values.
